item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The image that was used in this article is non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) as it's the copyrighted work of Tommy Ingberg.Thus, the image has been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Nice. The original version had many mistakes, but only the post-rewrite version matters right now, and I enjoyed it.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this quite a bit. More than I feel I should actually, haha. I do have an issue with something. In the earlier parts of the description of SCP-1403 it states that "Instances of SCP-1403 do not appear to consume food or produce waste,…" but then later on the article states "The creatures directly filter proteins from their environment into their blood through their gills. This process produces very little waste." This is either a personal issue and I just dislike the wording or something. I can see how "appear" could mean that it isn't visible to the naked eye. I also think that within the foundation not stating it in a way that makes it clear is still an oversight. Personal preference? I don't know but I would like to see some clarification simply because of the contradiction or possibility to misunderstand.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: First thought on seeing the picture — "poor fishies!". Then I read it and went "nifty". So you can have my upvote, as long as you promise to keep normal goldfish in nice big tanks, or a comfy pond.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I agree. This article would be much more interesting.

item.title: Up rate
item.content:encoded: This SCP was very interesting to me. I do no have a very good sense of critique, but I do like this article. When I started reading, I was instantly drawn in. The subject on this article was not something I would be interested in, but it was still great concept either way. I did find few small flaws, but were unnoticeable when one is reading the article. Great job on the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: i feel like this could be better if it was a factory or wondertainment scp. its certainly a lot more "Factory" than the factory makeup

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Minor problem: You say four instances have been terminated during testing, which would leave 27. Yet you have 26.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: We missed a number.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well obviously it went into the light. :-P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Please explain the following:
There were 31. Four were killed during testing. There are now 26.
31-4=27
Where'd the other one go?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have to give a good bit of credit to doomsniffer for giving me a big hand with the science. Without him I would still be buried in Wikipedia articles and scientific journals, struggling to figure out what's going on.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There's still a good few things I dislike, I think I did mention them when you were running it by chat. (example - the containment procedures won't require radiation protection, since the fish only radiates gamma rays if a certain current not essential for its survival is applied. i.e. at most, lead box for certain experiments) but well done. This article has improved a whole lot, and is no longer offensively technobabbly. =^.^=

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: After the rewrite, my problems with this have been removed. I'm iffy on the addendum formatting, but it's not really an issue to me. Downvote removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You had me until the mass-energy conversion. Vote withheld.

item.title: Re: I can't believe it's not --butter-- deleted
item.content:encoded: I'm not in a position to use chat right now, later this afternoon.

item.title: Re: I can't believe it's not --butter-- deleted
item.content:encoded:
Eh. I'm willing to cut you a little slack because there are people who upvoted it that should have known better, and should have told you this doesn't add up.

That said, I have outlined a lot of the nonsense you got there that needs off. Do you come by chat? If yes… I'd be willing to discuss ways of improving this there as well.

item.title: Re: I can't believe it's not --butter-- deleted
item.content:encoded:
I'm sorry? I've actually asked a person to help me with the science in this, considering I don't really know much about science, however I never received a reply from them. I then decided to go ahead with the SCP, do as much research as I could, and rely on the feedback of others to determine whether or not I was spouting bullshit. Apparently you're the first with some real scientific knowledge to take a look?

Anyway, I'm sorry you hate it so much. I'd be willing to change it to make more sense if I knew how.

item.title: I can't believe it's not --butter-- deleted
item.content:encoded:
Okay. I haven't read this when it was posted, but the science in this article is plain appalling.
Let's go through it fail by fail.

Yo, things this long should be in collapsibles.
Sincerely, Scantron

These entities do not eat or defecate, subsisting solely on electricity.

Electricity is not a word that appears in any scientific writing ever. I suspect you might have wanted to say electric current, but even that's slightly odd. I mean, the electrodes are immersed in a conductive medium of a far lower resistance than fucking fish. Of course, the fish could have unusual conductive properties but that needs to be actually said. We have done testing on some of them, haven't we? In retrospect , this bit might be harsh because as of itself , this wouldn't be so offensive.. it becomes in combination with everything else.

The creatures can be removed from the bulbs by dismantling the bulb; however, they will not live longer than 5 days outside their bulb, even while supplied with electricity.

Once again , do you supply the electricity in crates? Also, why do they die? Did anyone ever try keeping them alive, sealing them in a similar liquid, or whatever? What do they die of even?

Dissections have revealed that the creatures are not common goldfish, though they share 96% identical DNA.

That can be said of a man and a pig.
Hell, you share 98 % or so DNA with a chimp.

They possess no digestive tract, and in its place is an organ that is theorized to be responsible for converting electricity into sustenance for the creature.

Okay, wat. Were there experiments done? Does the organ produce glucose from CO2 and H2O when current is supplied? And where and how does it get other things needed for sustenance. Is the bulb fluid rich in proteins or at least elements proteins can be made from? Is that why they die when taken out?

It is probable that they were genetically engineered.

Genetic engineering isn't magic. Genetic engineering could create a chimera (that is, a being with genetic material from unrelated species) but this pushes the boundaries of known physics, much less biology. This is like pointing at the flower that gives Mario fireballs and saing "Oi, must be nanotechnology"

An instance of SCP-1403-B will function nearly the same inside a modified non-anomalous bulb as when contained in an anomalous bulb; however, the water must contain a small concentration of salt or a similar mineral to facilitate the electrical current's flow. The water contained in the objects is nearly pure water. It appears that the objects lower the electrical resistance of the water by an unknown means.

First of all, calling salt a mineral in this context is stupid. What you mean is that there's a minimum concentration of electrolytes that has to be maintained. A small quantity isn't technical language either - you should specify. Oh, and . . "nearly pure water" is unspecific as well. Oh, and … I'd have expected the fish to be the conductive element there, not salty water. And if the fish lowers the resistance of the water further… I'm wondering that it didn't starve yet.

Whenever an instance of SCP-1403-A is connected to electrical current through the two pins on its side, the object manifests its anomalous properties.

Unless the fish is more conductive than the watery medium , this won't work. Much like you can't short-circuit an appliance using a piece of wood.

The instance of SCP-1403-B within will glow with an orange bio-luminescence.

Bioluminiscence is a well known biological process that has nothing to do with electricity - it's done by oxidation of luciferin, a chemical produced by their bodies. Anglerfish use it to attract prey. The fish could well do it, but it likely wouldn't depend on whether they are wired, and likely wouldn't get more intense by time.
Well, it could, but the way you write it, I don't buy it at all.

When the electrical current reaches greater than 70 amps, the creature begin emitting gamma radiation as well as visible light.

Do you know what an amp is, how big it is? A transformer-run soldering iron has about 2-3 amps of current at a low voltage in the tip. An electric oven uses maybe 10-15A (<3kW , 240V). This is an insane current to put something the size of a lightbulb through - giving how the limits of water's resistance go (there's only so much dissolved electrolyte you can have) the water will boil and bulb explode in a short while.

At 400 amps, the creature undergoes a transformation. It will emit an intensely bright flash of light, exceeding 140,000 lux, and an accompanying burst of gamma and Cherenkov radiation. The creature then converts most its body into thermal energy, electrical energy, and radiation. How this conversion is accomplished is unknown.

AHAHAHA I know how this happened.
The scientist has melted down his whole rig trying to put 400A through a friggin' lightbulb with a fish in it. Then, to avoid a reprimand, he called it a property of the object.
The creature converted its body into thermal energy, electrical energy and radiation == it burned up.

In this state, the creature is designated as an instance of SCP-1403-B-1. The SCP still retains the same shape, but possess the ability to levitate. Spectral analysis of instances of SCP-1403-B-1 have revealed their internal structure to be composed of little more than a loose cloud of electrons, which maintain the creature's shape. Electrical and thermal energy is contained within this electron cloud by an unknown means.

This is technobabble worse than Star Trek:Voyager's . For one, it doesn't levitate, it just has negligible mass. For two, why isn't there any note so as to how can a loose cloud of electrons not disperse by essentially ionising surrounding air? What does the last sentence even fucking mean? Also, where are the atom nuclei gone? Is there a fish made of nuclei without electron shells running around somewhere? How would it even stay together?

While an instance of SCP-1403-B-1 is surrounded by air, the air within its electron cloud will become a plasma in a manner similar to lightning.

Lightning is current flowing through air. That's not what's happening here, isn't it.

Instances of SCP-1403-B-1 can pass through solid matter without any kind of resistance. Their passing through an object in this manner causes the object to become very hot, heating it to an average of 260 degrees centigrade.

Without resistance, heats the object up, it's made of electrons. This is a stealth pun, isn't it?

Unique radiation signatures resembling that of the creature have been recorded from just below the Earth's crust, in the upper layers of the mantle.

This is beyond bullshit.
EDIT: My friend has put this best. This is like looking at a coin at the bottom of a swimming pool while it's there, and noting that due to a pattern of micro-scratches on the surface, it must have come from a specific vending machine.

Congratulations, you have written my new most hated SCP on the site.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a very strange article, as it seems to get worse the further it goes.

The water contained in the anomalous light bulbs is nearly pure water. It appears that the objects lower the electrical resistance of the water by an unknown means.

Neither of these seems to be in tone, or even necessary. Of note is referring to it as "the anomalous light bulbs"; this is a no-no. Same with "the goldfish". They're not goldfish, the article even says so, they're SCP-1403-B.

Mostly though, it's the freaking Saiyan goldfish effect that gets me. Now, it's not all bad: that there's a few loose in the earth's crust is actually really great. I just feel like the actual effect is, in a word, ridiculous. Also, gamma radiation seems to be really popular right now. Just throwing that out there. Give them some kind of third-stage ethereal form, sure, to get that ending, but for the love of anything, not what you've done here.

item.title: Updated
item.content:encoded: I've just revised the article. The description of the energy-form of the fish has been changed to be a little more believable, and make a little more sense. The labels SCP-1403 and SCP-1403-1 have been replaced. They are now SCP-1403-A, SCP-1403-B, and SCP-1403-B-1, referring to the bulbs, the fish, and the energy-forms of the fish respectively.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the concept, but it's a bit dodgy at parts.

If they're genetically engineered, are they designed to turn into said energy-fish-thing, or is it a malfunction? Also, why the earth's mantle?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not sure on the first part, I like the second part. I'll include it in the rewrite of the transformation part.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm going to re-write that bit soon. I feel much the same as you about it. I'm trying to come up with a more plausible explanation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
contain medium to high levels of radiation, with appropriate shielding in place.

Shouldn't that say "high levels of gamma radiation"?

Instances of SCP-1403 include thirty one1 thirty light bulbs of non-standard design.

An instance of SCP-1403 will function nearly the same inside a modified non-anomalous bulb as when contained in an anomalous bulb, … The water contained in the anomalous light bulbs is nearly pure water. It appears that the objects lower the electrical resistance of the water by an unknown means.

Since the fish and the lightbulb are anomalous in different ways when seperated, I think that they should be given their own labels, like SCP-1403-A and SCP-1403-B, with the non-physical evolution of the fish being SCP-1403-B-1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think the concept needs to be simplified. You took what could have been a nice, simple oddity and tried to make something more out of it. Nothing wrong with that in and of itself, but this just falls short for me. No vote.

Not sure if I expressed my point clearly enough, but whatever.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You had a nice, simple object until you got into the increased power (why would anyone…) and the transformations. The second half of the article lacks anything resembling cohesion with everything else.

In short the hook feels pasted on and/or poorly improvised.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Changed title to Electric Goldfish.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Whereas I'm not fond enough to upvote, this has clearly improved from it's initial form. Well done thus far.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't like it. Some of the writing needs work and the idea isn't that interesting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks, the thing about replacing it with a regular goldfish is new. I added it after someone who proofread it asked me what would happen if you did that. I'm not sure who it was, it could have been Tprince. So many people gave me input on this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ah, I see. I'll fix that. Also, I've removed the reference to the Factory and the tag.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's what Forlorn was getting at. Visible light and gamma radiation are both on the electromagnetic spectrum, and he thought you ought to fix "light" to "visible light".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Okay, gotcha. And yeah, just eliminating the exact percentage would probably be better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No, gamma radiation is too high frequency to be visible to the human eye. It's commonly found nuclear fission reactors, and it will give you all kinds of cancer if it doesn't just kill you outright.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
When the electrical current reaches greater than 70 amps, the creature begin emitting gamma radiation as well as light.

Um… I might be mistaken, but gamma radiation is light, isn't it? If you meant “visible light”, could you perhaps clarify that?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "Oh yeah, have you heard about my new band, Electric Goldfish? They're a ska band."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Glad to see this is doing well. Would have flagged up the 'pure energy' thing if I'd noticed during proofreading: I see what you're going for and 'various forms' is probably as good as you're going to get without writing an entirely new paragraph explaining why they think it's energy despite having a (presumably) distinct shape. 'Electric Goldfish' sounds good for the title if you did want to change. I like the mention re replacing the goldfish with a regular one - is that new?
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: See though, I think the difference between a goldfish and a digestive system-less goldfish that feeds on electricity would be a bit bigger than that between a human and a chimp. I mean, a fish's digestive system has like five different organs in it, and while I get that a lot of a genome is junk DNA and regulating cellular activity, it just doesn't gel with me. The article would be better off not mentioning the percentage similarity and going with something like "analysis of the genome indicates that SCP-1403 is likely a genetically modified form of goldfish" or however you'd put it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I want to start a band called Electric Goldfish.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: In addition to making changes based on Incendio and Scantron's input, I'm considering changing the name to "Electric Goldfish".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Weeeeell, a 4% difference in DNA is actually pretty huge. Humans and chimps share about 96% DNA, if I recall correctly. Add in the fact that these are theoretically genetically engineered to feed on electricity, and I could see it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, doesn't seem very Factory to me. Factory items (almost) always have a functional value with an an appreciable risk/cost. Plus, I don't think I've ever seen a biological Factory item.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think this tries to do too many things at once. I'd see Conservation of WTF for a better explanation of this, but you should pick one or two really weird aspects of this that you'd like to emphasize and explore them. Also,

though they share 96% identical DNA. They possess no digestive tract

I get the weird feeling that you'd need to change more than 4% of something's DNA to get rid of a full digestive tract (although I might be wrong). Which is why that's where my suspension of disbelief failed. That, the aforementioned weirdness problems, and the Factory link are why I'm downvoting this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like :) some good ideas there, definitely.
The whole thing about 'converting their bodies into various forms of energy, yet still retaining their shape and possess the ability to levitate' seems a little bit whacky; partly due to 'forms of energy' being what: heat, electromagnetic, sound, kinetic, potential…
[it's like saying: "they convert their bodies into food" - well what kind of food? steak? bananas?]
I think a little bit more specific detail on this would clarify it.
Idea: the fish convert their bodies into various forms of energy, into an entity that has a 'shape' identified by a heat imprint/sound source/light source corresponding directly to the initial shape of the fish.

Might just be me being picky and stuff, that bit just seems slightly out of touch (as if you wrote it without any second thought) since everything else seems so scientifically explainable and believable.

+1 though, nice job!

item.title: Re: Interesting, but...
item.content:encoded: Or… just look it up in a dictionary? "Possess" is correct.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I remember seeing the image with the fish being "SCP Fuel." I was expecting something like this, but not bad.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: To me it made the whole thing a little more believable. Goldfish usually live in fresh water, which has a rather high resistance to electricity. Saltwater does better, but goldfish wouldn't live in it for very long.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm also not feeling this as factory.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Also, the details about how the bulbs maybe anomalously lower the resistance of water seems a bit unnecessary. What does that add, really?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm really not sure what to say on that. I'll just wait and see what others have to say about it.

item.title: Re: Interesting, but...
item.content:encoded: I'm just going to trust you're gotten it right until someone I trust more says you're wrong.

item.title: Re: Interesting, but...
item.content:encoded:
It should be possess. It wasn't, until I just fixed it.

There was also a note saying that do not "possess a digestive tract" or some such, which was spelled correctly.

item.title: Re: Interesting, but...
item.content:encoded: Funny thing, that's how I had it the first time around, as "posses", but someone I asked to proofread it said it should be "possess".

item.title: Re: Interesting, but...
item.content:encoded:
On proofreading:

posses the ability to levitate

Note: I fixed this just before posting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not sure why you need the factory reference here. It doesn't really strike me as factory, and seems more just like a one sentence connection to increase the popularity a bit. As well, the sentence "It is probable that they were genetically engineered." is unnecessary

item.title: Re: Interesting, but...
item.content:encoded:
I changed "pure energy" to "various forms of energy", and changed █ fatalities to 3 fatalities. I argue that the number of years the specimens have been in containment need to remain censored because it would make censoring the date of their recovery a moot point.

As for proofreading, I've had quite a few people proofread this before it was even posted.

item.title: Interesting, but...
item.content:encoded:
pure energy

Fix that. There is no such thing, and it won't be long before someone less friendly than I brings it up.

in containment for ██ years and show no signs of deteriorating.

Unneeded black box. Trust me, I've been shot down for the same thing.

6 serious injuries and █ fatalities

Equally unneeded and relatively unambiguous - it's 1 to 9. And the number of deaths is not a secret, especially if injuries are listed.

Aside from that, needs some proofreading.
